bids.reasoning: The opponent's opening bid gives me all valueF, which is the worst possible outcome for me (utility = 0.2 across all issues). Their claim that it's "balanced" is clearly false - it's maximally bad for me. This is t=0.00, so I should open with my best bid (all valueA) to anchor high and signal my preferences. There's no pressure to concede yet.

The opponent is claiming their all-valueF offer is "balanced" and "mutually rewarding," but it gives me minimum utility (0.2 on all issues, total utility ≈ 0.2). This is clearly a very aggressive opening that favors them. Meanwhile, I opened with all-valueA (maximum utility for me). We're very early at t=0.02, so I should hold firm near my top bid. The opponent has repeated the exact same bid twice with no concession, so I should maintain a strong position and not concede yet.

The opponent is clearly trying to lowball me with all valueF offers (utility ≈ 0.2 on each issue), claiming it's "balanced" while it's clearly the worst outcome for me. Their "concession" on issueA from valueF to valueE is minimal. We're very early (t=0.04), so I should hold firm near my optimal bid. I'll maintain my top utility bid (all valueA) to signal I won't accept these poor offers.

We're very early in the negotiation (t=0.06). The opponent is making extremely low offers (all valueF, with one tiny move to valueE on issueA), which gives me very low utility (~0.2 across the board). Their messages claim "balanced" and "fair" but the numbers are completely lopsided in their favor. I should hold firm near my top utility bid. I'll stick with my all-valueA bid since we're still in early game and the opponent has barely conceded anything.

It's still very early (t=0.08). The opponent is clearly making very low offers (mostly valueF for everything, which is the worst for me), while claiming fairness - their words contradict their bids entirely. They've made tiny moves: issueA went to valueE in t=0.04-0.06, then back to valueF with issueB moving to valueE at t=0.08. This is extremely slow concession behavior. I should maintain my high-utility bid while possibly making a tiny concession on the least important issue (issueD, weight 0.1) to signal some willingness to negotiate, without giving up much.

We're at t=0.1, still early in the negotiation. The opponent's offers have all been very low utility for me (all F values, with minor moves to E on some issues). I should continue holding close to my top-utility bid but can make a small concession on the least important issue (issueD) to signal some willingness to move.
